# Firmware Output Files

This document describes the firmware output files generated during compilation.

## Generated Files

### Build Output (`build/` directory)

- **`firmware.elf`** - ELF executable file
  - Format: ELF32-little-RISC-V
  - Contains: Complete executable with debug symbols
  - Usage: For debugging and uploading to target

- **`firmware.bin`** - Raw binary file
  - Format: Binary
  - Contains: Raw machine code
  - Usage: Direct memory programming

- **`firmware.hex`** - Intel HEX format
  - Format: Intel HEX
  - Contains: Machine code in HEX format
  - Usage: Flash programming tools

- **`firmware.dis`** - Disassembly listing
  - Format: Text
  - Contains: Assembly disassembly (without source)
  - Usage: Code analysis

### Firmware Output Files (`firmware_output/` directory)

- **`firmware_output/firmware.verilog`** - Verilog memory initialization file
  - Format: Verilog memory format
  - Contains: Firmware code formatted for Verilog memory initialization
  - Address: Starts at `@80000000` (Instruction RAM base)
  - Usage: For Verilog simulation and FPGA memory initialization
  - Generated by: `objcopy -O verilog`

- **`firmware_output/firmware.dump`** - Source-interleaved disassembly
  - Format: Text
  - Contains: Disassembly with source code interleaved (`-S` flag)
  - Usage: Debugging and code analysis
  - Generated by: `objdump -S`

- **`firmware_output/riscv32KC2_instr.verilog`** - Processed Verilog instruction memory file
  - Format: Verilog memory format
  - Contains: Same as firmware.verilog but with `@8` replaced by `@0`
  - Address: Starts at `@00000000` (after processing)
  - Usage: For Verilog simulation and FPGA instruction memory initialization
  - Generated by: `conv_firmware.bat` or `compile_manual.bat` post-processing

## Generation

### Automatic Generation

The files are automatically generated when you run:
```batch
compile_manual.bat
```

This will generate:
- All build output files in `build/` directory
- `firmware.verilog`, `firmware.dump`, and `riscv32KC2_instr.verilog` in `firmware_output/` directory (if tools available)

### Manual Generation

To generate only the Verilog and dump files:
```batch
conv_firmware.bat
```

This script:
1. Creates `firmware_output/` directory if it doesn't exist
2. Converts `build/firmware.elf` to `firmware_output/firmware.verilog`
3. Generates `firmware_output/firmware.dump` with source interleaved
4. Creates `firmware_output/riscv32KC2_instr.verilog` with `@8` â†’ `@0` replacement

## Tools Used

The conversion uses tools from `software/tools/`:
- **`objcopy.exe`** - Object file converter
  - Command: `objcopy -O verilog firmware.elf firmware.verilog`
  
- **`objdump.exe`** - Object file dumper
  - Command: `objdump -S firmware.elf > firmware.dump`

## Verilog Format

The `firmware.verilog` file uses the standard Verilog memory initialization format:

```
@<address>
<hex_bytes>
@<next_address>
<hex_bytes>
...
```

Example:
```
@80000000
97 11 00 20 93 81 81 A6 17 01 00 40 13 01 81 FF
...
```

- `@80000000` - Memory address (Instruction RAM base)
- Hex bytes - Machine code in hexadecimal

## Memory Map

The firmware is organized according to the AI RISC-V KC32 memory map:

- **Instruction RAM**: `0x80000000` - `0x9FFFFFFF`
  - Code sections (.text, .init, etc.)
  - Read-only data (.rodata)

- **Data RAM**: `0x90000000` - `0xAFFFFFFF`
  - Initialized data (.data)
  - Uninitialized data (.bss)
  - Stack

## Usage in Verilog Simulation

To use `firmware_output/firmware.verilog` in Verilog simulation:

```verilog
// In your memory module
initial begin
    $readmemh("firmware_output/firmware.verilog", memory_array);
end
```

Or for the instruction memory version:
```verilog
initial begin
    $readmemh("firmware_output/riscv32KC2_instr.verilog", memory_array);
end
```

## File Sizes

Typical file sizes (may vary):
- `firmware.elf`: ~12-15 KB (with debug symbols)
- `firmware.bin`: ~3-4 KB (code + data)
- `firmware.hex`: ~5-6 KB (text format)
- `firmware.verilog`: ~3-4 KB (memory initialization)
- `firmware.dump`: ~50-100 KB (disassembly with source)

## Notes

- The Verilog file uses addresses starting at `0x80000000` (Instruction RAM)
- The processed version replaces `@7` with `@0` for compatibility with certain hardware
- All addresses are absolute (no address translation)
- The dump file includes both assembly and source code for easier debugging
